content.text:
*"Let all things be done decently and in order."*{1 Corinthians 14:40}{*"Symmetry is what we see at a glance."*}{Blaise Pascal}

*"Symmetry is what we see at a glance."*{— Blaise Pascal}

A **permutation** is a bijection from a set to itself—a rearrangement 
that moves elements around while keeping the set intact. The set of all 
permutations of a given set forms a group under composition, and this 
group captures the full symmetry of the set considered as an unstructured 
collection of points.

content.title: Symmetric Group
content.content:
Let $A$ be a nonempty set. The **symmetric group** 
on $A$, denoted $S_A$, is the set of all bijections $\sigma: A \to A$ 
under the operation of function composition.

When $A = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, we write $S_n$ and call it the 
**symmetric group on $n$ letters**.

content.title: $S_n$ is a Group
content.content: The symmetric group $S_n$ is a group of order $n!$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
**Closure:** If $\sigma, \tau \in S_n$, then $\sigma \circ \tau$ is 
a bijection (composition of bijections).

**Associativity:** Function composition is associative.

**Identity:** The identity function $\iota(x) = x$ satisfies 
$\sigma \circ \iota = \iota \circ \sigma = \sigma$.

**Inverses:** Every bijection has an inverse function that is also 
a bijection.

**Order:** The number of bijections from an $n$-set to itself is 
$n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2) \cdots 2 \cdot 1 = n!$.

content.text:
A permutation $\sigma \in S_n$ can be written in **two-row 
notation**:

content.title: A permutation in $S_4$
content.problem:
Let $\sigma \in S_4$ be defined by $\sigma(1) = 2$, $\sigma(2) = 4$, 
$\sigma(3) = 1$, $\sigma(4) = 3$. In two-row notation:
\[
 \sigma = \begin{pmatrix}
 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
 2 & 4 & 1 & 3
 \end{pmatrix}
\]

content.text:
We compose permutations by applying them in sequence, reading 
**right to left** (following function composition convention).

content.title: Composing permutations
content.problem:
Let $\sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ and 
$\tau = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ in $S_3$.

To find $\sigma \circ \tau$, we compute $(\sigma \circ \tau)(k) = 
\sigma(\tau(k))$:
$$
 (\sigma \circ \tau)(1) &= \sigma(\tau(1)) = \sigma(1) = 2 \\
 (\sigma \circ \tau)(2) &= \sigma(\tau(2)) = \sigma(3) = 1 \\
 (\sigma \circ \tau)(3) &= \sigma(\tau(3)) = \sigma(2) = 3
$$
Thus $\sigma \circ \tau = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 
\end{pmatrix}$.

content.content:
Be careful with order! In general, $\sigma \circ \tau \neq \tau \circ 
\sigma$. The symmetric group $S_n$ is **non-abelian** for $n \geq 3$.

content.title: The groups $S_1$, $S_2$, $S_3$
content.problem:
- $S_1 = \{\iota\}$ has order $1$ (trivial group).

- $S_2 = \{\iota, (1\ 2)\}$ has order $2$ (isomorphic to $\Z_2$).

- $S_3$ has order $6$ and is the smallest non-abelian group.

items: Compute all elements of $S_3$ in two-row notation.

Show that $S_3$ is non-abelian by finding $\sigma, \tau \in S_3$ 
 with $\sigma\tau \neq \tau\sigma$.
Prove that $S_n$ is non-abelian for all $n \geq 3$.

marginNotes.author: Augustin-Louis Cauchy
marginNotes.text:
The systematic study of permutations 
began with his foundational work in the early 19th century.
